metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion verifying the rubric contains the exact `## Finding threshold` heading, with a corresponding `-k finding_threshold` test in `tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py`.
evidence: Approach: 'The rubric body uses these exact second-level headings so tests have a mechanical oracle: `## Finding threshold` …'
No AC references 'Finding threshold' or a test function checking for this heading
ACs 7-10 cover Required finding fields, Supported client-shape matrix, and the two Non-findings rules — but not Finding threshold
findings.finding: The Approach requires the rubric to contain a `## Finding threshold` second-level heading (listed first among the four required headings), but no acceptance criterion verifies its presence. This is a described deliverable with no covering criterion.
findings.location: Approach — 'The rubric body uses these exact second-level headings' vs. Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
scenarios: Implementer omits the Finding threshold section from the rubric; no test catches the omission; the rubric violates the Approach spec silently
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] The rubric contains the exact `## Finding threshold` second-level heading. Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py -k finding_threshold`.'

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the `Approach` section, extend the `Supported client-shape matrix` specification to require that the rubric body enumerates the concrete dimension values under each label (not just the label headings), so the AC `client_shape_matrix` test or rubric content requirement covers value presence, not just heading presence.
evidence: Plan text: 'Under `Supported client-shape matrix`, require the literal labels `Harness`, `Target project`, `Platform and venue`, and `Project location and access`.' — only label presence is required, not content under each label.
AC `client_shape_matrix` test confirms only the four literal dimension LABELS — no test checks for enumerated values beneath each heading.

Parent epic jira-reb-1003 carries the concrete values ('Ruby, Python, Java, Next.js, .NET', 'macOS, Windows, Linux, BSD', etc.) but this child plan does not mandate those values appear in the rubric body.
The processing rule that determines when to fire ('a materially different supported client shape') is meaningless to the LLM without an enumeration of what client shapes are supported.
findings.finding: The `Supported client-shape matrix` section is defined by four bare label strings (`Harness`, `Target project`, `Platform and venue`, `Project location and access`) but the plan never specifies what VALUES must appear under each label in the rubric body. The AC test `client_shape_matrix` only asserts the four label strings are present — a rubric with four empty-body headings passes the test. Without enumerated matrix values the LLM executing the criterion has no basis for knowing which specific shapes constitute 'supported clients', making the decision rule (fire when a plan breaks a supported client shape) unexecutable from the rubric text alone.
findings.location: Approach section — 'Under `Supported client-shape matrix`, require the literal labels `Harness`, `Target project`, `Platform and venue`, and `Project location and access`' / AC line: `client_shape_matrix`
scenarios: A rubric author writes four section headings with no enumerated values beneath them — all AC tests pass, but the criterion fires inconsistently or not at all because the LLM must guess what 'Harness' includes.

A plan that breaks Windows support is missed because the rubric does not list Windows under `Platform and venue`, leaving the LLM to rely on world-knowledge rather than a contract-bearing specification.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Approach section: 'Under each label, enumerate the specific supported values: under `Harness` list Python library / CLI / remote MCP; under `Target project` list Ruby / Python / Java / Next.js / .NET / Terraform-in-monorepo; under `Platform and venue` list macOS / Windows / Linux / BSD across CI, servers, and developer workstations; under `Project location and access` list in-checkout CWD / explicitly located workspace / server execution outside checkout.' Update AC `client_shape_matrix` to assert at least one concrete value per dimension label is present in the rubric.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief CLAUDE.md entry or ADR capturing why `project.portability` lives in the project overlay (not as a built-in criterion), so the 'project-as-client' validation intent is preserved in the repository for future contributors.
evidence: 'Use the existing project overlay seam rather than adding a built-in criterion. This keeps rebar-specific policy in the rebar repository while exercising the same supported extension boundary available to clients.'
No mention of CLAUDE.md update, ADR, or design-doc change anywhere in the plan.

T5e rubric: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / CLAUDE.md / design doc update. SEVERITY: a new pipeline stage or cross-component coupling with no doc/ADR is MAJOR on documentation.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — the project consuming its own client-extension overlay seam for project-specific criteria rather than adding a built-in criterion — but does not update CLAUDE.md, an ADR, or any design doc to capture this rationale in the repository. Once the ticket is closed, the reasoning ('keeps rebar-specific policy in the rebar repository while exercising the same supported extension boundary available to clients') exists only in the Jira ticket, leaving future contributors without in-repo guidance and at risk of 'fixing' the overlay entry into a built-in criterion.
scenarios: A future contributor sees `project.portability` in the overlay and, not knowing why it isn't a built-in criterion, moves it into the core criteria registry — breaking the validation of the extension seam and the separation of rebar policy from framework code.
A client adopting the overlay extension pattern looks for documented examples in the repo and finds none, reducing their confidence in the pattern's stability.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to CLAUDE.md (or a new `docs/adr/NNN-project-portability-overlay.md`) with one paragraph: explain that `project.portability` is intentionally kept in `.rebar/criteria_routing.json` (the project overlay) rather than the built-in criteria registry, both to keep rebar-specific policy out of the framework and to continuously validate the client extension seam that other consumers rely on.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling behavior when the prompt file is missing or has malformed front matter (e.g., log a warning and skip the criterion, or raise a typed exception that the fan-in runner catches and surfaces without aborting other criteria).
evidence: Plan states 'The project rubric resolves through the production prompt loader for project.portability' but never specifies what the loader does when the file is absent or malformed
No retry, fallback, or error-surfacing behavior described for this read path
Live LLM behavior is explicitly out of scope, but file-I/O failure handling is also unaddressed
findings.finding: The plan adds a runtime dependency on a new prompt file (`.rebar/prompts/plan-review-project-portability.md`) resolved through the production prompt loader, but specifies no error handling for the case where that file is missing, has malformed YAML front matter, or fails to parse. The failure mode and any graceful-degradation path are unspecified.
scenarios: Prompt file is accidentally deleted or not deployed — criterion silently fails to execute or throws an unhandled exception
Front matter is malformed YAML — loader raises a parse error with no graceful degradation, potentially breaking other criteria in the same pass
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, add: 'If the prompt file is absent or unparseable, the prompt loader must raise a typed `PromptResolutionError`; the fan-in runner catches it, logs the criterion id and file path at ERROR level, and excludes the criterion from the batch result rather than aborting the run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify at minimum a structured log line emitted at INFO level when `project.portability` is routed and at ERROR/WARN level when it fails to load or fan-in, so operators can detect and debug criterion failures in production.
evidence: Plan describes tests with 'deterministic fakes' and defers live behavior to a calibration child, but specifies no logging, metrics, or alerts for production runtime
No mention of what is emitted when routing, prompt resolution, or fan-in fails for this criterion
findings.finding: No observability instrumentation (log, metric, or trace) is specified for the new `project.portability` criterion's execution path. If the criterion fails to load, route, or fan-in at runtime, operators have no signal.
scenarios: Criterion silently stops executing after a deployment change — no alert fires, no log line is emitted, portability failures go undetected indefinitely
Fan-in runner drops the criterion's findings due to a configuration error — silent data loss with no operator visibility

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the safe partial-state behavior: either (a) specify that the prompt loader's absence of a file for a routed criterion is handled gracefully (skip + warn), or (b) add a guard in the routing registry that validates prompt file existence at activation time and refuses to activate a criterion whose prompt is missing.
evidence: AC item 11 covers 'existing behavior remains unchanged when the project overlay is absent' but does not cover the case where the routing entry is present and the prompt file is absent
The plan lists two separate deliverables (routing JSON entry and prompt file) with no atomic deployment guarantee or partial-state guard
findings.finding: The plan does not address the partial-deployment or rollback state where the routing entry for `project.portability` exists in `criteria_routing.json` but the prompt file has not yet been committed (or has been rolled back). Consumer behavior in this intermediate state is unspecified.
scenarios: A partial merge lands the routing entry before the prompt file — the routing registry resolves the criterion but the prompt loader fails, leaving the system in an error state
A rollback removes the prompt file but not the routing entry — every subsequent pass attempt for portability throws or silently drops

findings.finding: AC #6 instructs the implementer to run `pytest tests/unit/test_project_criteria_overlay.py tests/unit/test_plan_review_production_runner.py` to prove existing behavior is unchanged. Neither file exists in the repo. The actual overlay tests are in `tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py` and the actual production runner tests are in `tests/unit/workflow/test_production_batch_runner.py`. Referencing non-existent files as regression guards means the AC is unrunnable as written and the regression check would silently fail (pytest would error on the missing path).

AC6 names two test files that do not exist in the repo: `tests/unit/test_project_criteria_overlay.py` and `tests/unit/test_plan_review_production_runner.py`. The actual existing analogs are `tests/unit/test_criteria_overlay.py` and `tests/unit/workflow/test_production_batch_runner.py` respectively. Running the AC6 pytest command as written would error out with 'file not found', not provide the intended regression guard.

AC6 references `tests/unit/test_plan_review_production_runner.py` as a file to run, but this file does not exist; the actual production runner test file is `tests/unit/workflow/test_production_batch_runner.py`. An implementer following AC6 literally would either create a misnamed new file or fail to locate the existing coverage they are meant to leave unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align AC10's description with the Approach's mandated literal: replace 'the exact rule allowing specialization behind…' with 'the exact rule `Project-specific behavior behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary is allowed`'.
evidence: Approach: 'require the literal rules … `Project-specific behavior behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary is allowed`'
AC10: 'the exact rule allowing specialization behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary'
The word 'specialization' is absent from the Approach's mandated literal
findings.finding: AC10 describes the project_configuration_guard test as checking 'the exact rule allowing specialization behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary,' but the Approach mandates the literal string 'Project-specific behavior behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary is allowed.' The word 'specialization' appears in AC10 but nowhere in the Approach's quoted literal, creating terminology drift that could send an implementer to assert the wrong string.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC10 (project_configuration_guard) vs. Approach — Non-findings bullet
scenarios: Implementer reads AC10 and asserts 'specialization' appears in the rubric; rubric contains 'Project-specific behavior' instead; test passes or fails incorrectly depending on which text was authored
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC10 to: 'The rubric contains the exact rule `Project-specific behavior behind project configuration or an explicit extension boundary is allowed`. Prove with `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py -k project_configuration_guard`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Align the leaf's routing/fan-in ACs with the parent's verification key: either name the combined test function `test_routing_and_fan_in` (covering leaf scope, container scope, and production fan-in together) so the parent's `-k routing_and_fan_in` command collects it, or update the parent epic's AC 3 verify command before merging this leaf.
evidence: Parent AC (jira-reb-1003): 'proves both leaf and container plans route through production project fan-in. Verify: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py -k routing_and_fan_in`'

Leaf ACs use three distinct `-k` flags: `leaf_routing`, `container_routing`, `production_fan_in` — none of which contains the substring `routing_and_fan_in` that pytest's `-k` filter requires to select the parent's compound gate.
The parent's verify command would collect zero tests if the leaf's naming is followed, making the gate vacuously pass.
findings.finding: The leaf splits the parent's single compound AC (`-k routing_and_fan_in`) into three separate test-key ACs (`-k leaf_routing`, `-k container_routing`, `-k production_fan_in`). If the authored test file does not contain a function or class whose name contains `routing_and_fan_in`, the parent's verification command passes vacuously with zero collected tests — silently dropping the parent's acceptance gate while appearing green.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC lines for `leaf_routing`, `container_routing`, `production_fan_in` vs. parent epic AC: 'Verify: `.venv/bin/pytest -q tests/unit/test_project_portability_criterion.py -k routing_and_fan_in`'
scenarios: A CI run of the parent's AC verification command collects 0 tests and exits 0, giving a false green on the routing gate while the three fine-grained tests pass under a separate command the parent never specified.
A reviewer re-checking the parent's ACs against the merged leaf finds the parent's compound gate can never be satisfied without a function whose name matches `routing_and_fan_in`.
findings.suggested_fix: Rename the three split test functions to a single `test_routing_and_fan_in` that exercises leaf scope, container scope, and production fan-in as sub-assertions, preserving the parent's verify command; or, if the three-function split is preferred, update the parent AC's verify clause to `-k 'leaf_routing or container_routing or production_fan_in'` and re-attest the parent first.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
